blocks.text: a day of darkness and gloom, a day of clouds and thick darkness! Like blackness spread upon the mountains a great and powerful army comes; their like has never been from of old, nor will be again after them in ages to come.
1–2 : (see 1.15n.), even though catastrophe has already struck. is an anticipated feature of the (see also Am 5.18; Zeph 1.15), highlighted in the advent of a .
Fire devours in front of them, and behind them a flame burns. Before them the land is like the garden of Eden, but after them a desolate wilderness, and nothing escapes them.
They have the appearance of horses, and like war-horses they charge.
3–4 : The scene the onslaught (2.3,6,10), (see Gen 2.8–9; 13.10; Isa 51.3), stands in contrast with the devastation afterward, .
As with the rumbling of chariots, they leap on the tops of the mountains, like the crackling of a flame of fire devouring the stubble, like a powerful army drawn up for battle.
Before them peoples are in anguish, all faces grow pale.b
Like warriors they charge, like soldiers they scale the wall. Each keeps to its own course, they do not swerve from their paths.
5–7 : The similes and make it clear that locusts, not an army with chariots, are meant.
They do not jostle one another, each keeps to its own track; they burst through the weapons and are not halted.
They leap upon the city, they run upon the walls; they climb up into the houses, they enter through the windows like a thief.
The earth quakes before them, the heavens tremble. The sun and the moon are darkened, and the stars withdraw their shining.
The Lord utters his voice at the head of his army; how vast is his host! Numberless are those who obey his command. Truly the day of the Lord is great; terrible indeed—who can endure it?

10–11 : is typical of a theophany (e.g., Ps 77.18), as is the darkening of astral objects (cf. Isa 13.10). God is the general of , which may describe the locusts, and thus explains why the locusts are related to .
Yet even now, says the Lord, return to me with all your heart, with fasting, with weeping, and with mourning;
rend your hearts and not your clothing. Return to the Lord, your God, for he is gracious and merciful, slow to anger, and abounding in steadfast love, and relents from punishing.

12–13 : Language from ch 1 reappears, calling for a ritual response. The reference to reflects Deuteronomic idioms (Deut 10.16; 30.2). The characterization of the in v. 13b appears elsewhere, though Joel omits mention of God's judgment (see, e.g., Ex 34.6–7; Num 14.18).
Who knows whether he will not turn and relent, and leave a blessing behind him, a grain offering and a drink offering for the Lord, your God?
14 : , cf. Jon 3.9. may leave an offering for himself.
Blow the trumpet in Zion; sanctify a fast; call a solemn assembly;
gather the people. Sanctify the congregation; assemble the aged; gather the children, even infants at the breast. Let the bridegroom leave his room, and the bride her canopy.

Between the vestibule and the altar let the priests, the ministers of the Lord, weep. Let them say, “Spare your people, O Lord, and do not make your heritage a mockery, a byword among the nations. Why should it be said among the peoples, ‘Where is their God?’”
1.2–2.17: Crisis and call to action. A series of staccato-like poems, some of which depict the crisis (e.g., 1.17–18), more of which call people to act (e.g., 1.14).
2.1–17: Though similar to ch 1, these poems focus more on describing the calamity than commanding a response.

17 : refers to the entry hall of the Temple (see 1 Kings 6.3). The was in the Temple courtyard. The priests, stationed between the altar and the vestibule, are to utter the prayer given to them by Joel. Cf. Ex 32.12; Deut 9.26–28 for similar logic.
Then the Lord became jealous for his land, and had pity on his people.
In response to his people the Lord said: I am sending you grain, wine, and oil, and you will be satisfied; and I will no more make you a mockery among the nations.

18–19 : , better “zealous”; cf. Zech 1.14; Isa 9.7. God's first concern, even before the people, was (see 1.6–7n.). , mentioned as absent in 1.10. The reference to suggests that God responded directly to the prayer in 2.17.

I will remove the northern army far from you, and drive it into a parched and desolate land, its front into the eastern sea, and its rear into the western sea; its stench and foul smell will rise up. Surely he has done great things!
20 : is probably an allusion to the mythic enemy from the north (cf. Jer 1.13–15; 4.6; 6.1). , the Dead Sea; , the Mediterranean. may reflect the plague traditions, cf. Ex 7.18; 8.14.
Do not fear, O soil; be glad and rejoice, for the Lord has done great things!
21 : The land is now personified as .
Do not fear, you animals of the field, for the pastures of the wilderness are green; the tree bears its fruit, the fig tree and vine give their full yield.
22 : The imagery represents a reversal of the earlier situation. will have pastureland (cf. 1.18), and and will bear fruit (cf. 1.12).
O children of Zion, be glad and rejoice in the Lord your God; for he has given the early rain for your vindication, he has poured down for you abundant rain, the early and the later rain, as before.
23 : replaces the earlier drought (1.20). , cf. Ps 149.2. Human life focuses on Jerusalem. rain, in late fall; , in early spring.
The threshing floors shall be full of grain, the vats shall overflow with wine and oil.
I will repay you for the years that the swarming locust has eaten, the hopper, the destroyer, and the cutter, my great army, which I sent against you.
25 : often has legal connotations; cf. Ex 21.34. is much longer than the attack earlier described. The four forms of locust appear here in a different order from that in 1.4.
You shall eat in plenty and be satisfied, and praise the name of the Lord your God, who has dealt wondrously with you. And my people shall never again be put to shame.
You shall know that I am in the midst of Israel, and that I, the Lord, am your God and there is no other. And my people shall never again be put to shame.

2.18–27: The Lord responds. Since the deity responds, one may infer that the people, especially the priests, complied with Joel's commands, though the book does not report the people's actual response to Joel's admonitions.
26–27 : The promise of restoration is confirmed by allusion to the covenantal relationship with the only true God; cf. Isa 45.5,6,18.
Then afterward I will pour out my spirit on all flesh; your sons and your daughters shall prophesy, your old men shall dream dreams, and your young men shall see visions.

2.28 : here implies enabling various forms of prophetic revelation (cf. Ezek 39.29). What had been limited to the house of David (Zech 12.10) or the prophets is now available to all Israel. Cf. Acts 2.16–21.
Even on the male and female slaves, in those days, I will pour out my spirit.
I will show portents in the heavens and on the earth, blood and fire and columns of smoke.
The sun shall be turned to darkness, and the moon to blood, before the great and terrible day of the Lord comes.

Then everyone who calls on the name of the Lord shall be saved; for in Mount Zion and in Jerusalem there shall be those who escape, as the Lord has said, and among the survivors shall be those whom the Lord calls.
2.28–3.21. A future of well-being for Israel. Poetic and prose oracles focusing on Israel and their human enemies. God will again bring victory that will result in marvelous fertility.

30–32 : These verses are probably poetic. are cosmic events. On the sky darkening, cf. Isa 13.10; 34.4; Jer 4.23; Ezek 32.7–8; Am 8.9. In Joel 2.10, the locusts had darkened the sky; now God is the cause. , i.e., worships the . and probably refer to those not killed in an eschatological battle; cf. Zech 14.16, which includes non-Israelites.
